content.full_text:
FPPC

Aug 23 360 S. Euclid Ave., #115
Pasadena, Ca. 91101
August 15, 1990

Fair Political Practices Commission -- Legal Section
428 J St., Suite 800
Sacramento, Ca. 95814

Dear Sirs:

At the suggestion of one of your attorneys (John Wallace), I
am requesting expedited formal written advice concerning a slate
mailer which is under consideration for this November's election.

We are considering one or several mass mailings which would
feature joint recommendations by four candidates or officeholders:
one federal, two state, and one local officeholder. The local
officeholder is not up for reelection; the other officeholders are
candidates this November. The mailings will go to voters in a
large region, including but extending well beyond any one
officeholder's own district. The mailing will be organized and
produced by a consulting group, at the behest of the recommending
officeholders, and the consultants will earn a profit through this
activity. These four officeholders or candidates will make
recommendations concerning statewide partisan races, state
legislative races, possibly local races, and statewide and local
ballot measures. None of the four officeholders control committees
for any of the ballot measures on which recommendations will be
made. At least four state candidates will be included among those
candidates or issues recommended.

Our primary concern centers around the potential for in-kind
contributions by the slate mailing to either those candidates
making recommendations or those candidates being recommended. I am
pleased that the Commission seems to be moving toward the "express
advocacy" standard. To me, that approach makes the most sense.
Therefore, I would like to outline the three alternative financing
arrangements we are considering and how we plan to interpret
Commission regulations. Since we may move quickly to implement
these plans, we would appreciate an expedited response if the
Commission believes there might be significant problems with our
interpretation.

Our first approach would be to have some or all of those
candidates or measures being recommended (the recommendees) pay for
the mailing. Their shares would be calculated through a
combination of physical space and prominence on the mailing, and
the size of their districts. Those officeholders featured on the
card as making these recommendations (the recommendors) would not
be charged. (However, some recommendors may also be recommendees,
in which case they would pay a fair share, calculated in the same
way as for all other recommendees.) Those recommendees paying for
the mailing would be appropriately identified on the mailing.

Under this approach, we believe no in-kind contribution would
accrue to the recommenders. Nor would any in-kind contribution
accrue to any of recommendees; those who are paying for the mailing
would be appropriately identified, and those who are not may still
be included on the slate mailing at the discretion of the slate
committee, given the size of the slate.

A second approach would be to have the recommenders pay the
full cost of the mailing. In this case as well, we believe no
in-kind contribution would accrue to any recommendees, based on the
same slate mailer exemption.

Finally, a combination of the above two approaches may be
used, with both recommenders and recommendees providing funding.
Once again, we believe no in-kind contribution would accrue to
either recommenders or recommendees.

I hope the above information is sufficient to allow you to
make an informed judgment about our interpretation of the current
FEC regulations. I look forward to hearing from your office
shortly. If you need any additional information, please feel free
to call me at home at (818) 449-7655 or at work at (213) 414-6915.

Thank you for your assistance.

Sincerely yours,

Jonathan S. Fuhrman
Jonathan S. Fuhrman

sections.question: Are there potential in-kind contributions by a slate mailer to candidates making recommendations or candidates being recommended, and which of three proposed financing arrangements would comply with FPPC regulations?
sections.question_synthetic: The requester seeks expedited formal written advice on whether three proposed slate mailer financing arrangements (payment by recommendees, payment by recommendors, or a combination) would violate in-kind contribution rules under FPPC regulations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. No formal question/conclusion sections with headers are present. The legal question and proposed financing arrangements are embedded in the letter narrative. The document contains specific financing proposals (three alternatives) rather than seeking general guidance. Extraction confidence reduced slightly due to lack of formal Q/C structure and some document OCR quality issues in the letterhead.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Are there potential in-kind contributions by a slate mailer to candidates making recommendations or candidates being recommended, and which of three proposed financing arrangements would comply with FPPC regulations?

embedding.summary: A consultant group requests expedited FPPC advice on three financing arrangements for a multi-candidate slate mailer featuring joint recommendations by federal, state, and local officeholders, seeking clarification on whether any arrangement would constitute improper in-kind contributions under the slate mailer exemption.
